# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Parametric Form of a B-Spline Curve (Foundation of NURBS)

A B-spline curve of order $p$ with control points $\mathbf{P}_0, \mathbf{P}_1, \dots, \mathbf{P}_n$ and a knot vector $\mathbf{U} = \{u_0, u_1, \dots, u_{n+p+1}\}$ is defined as:

$$
\mathbf{C}(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u)\,\mathbf{P}_i,\qquad u \in [u_{p},\, u_{n+1}]
$$

where $N_{i,p}(u)$ is the B-spline basis function of order $p$.

### Step 1 — Start with the piecewise constant (order 0) basis.

For $p = 0$ the basis is:

$$
N_{i,0}(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } u_i \le u < u_{i+1} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This is simply a step function — the control point is "active" only over one knot interval.

### Step 2 — Promote to linear (order 1) by convex combination.

$$
N_{i,1}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+1} - u_i} N_{i,0}(u) + \frac{u_{i+2} - u}{u_{i+2} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,0}(u)
$$

This produces a triangular "hat" function — linearly blending between two adjacent control points.

### Step 3 — Generalise to arbitrary order using the Cox–de Boor recursion.

$$
N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+p} - u_i}\,N_{i,p-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+p+1} - u}{u_{i+p+1} - u_{i+1}}\,N_{i+1,p-1}(u)
$$

**Why it works:** Each basis function is a *convex combination* of the lower-order ones, so $\sum_i N_{i,p}(u) = 1$ for all $u$ — the *partition of unity* property that guarantees affine invariance.

### Step 4 — Convert to a tensor-product surface by sweeping one curve along another.

$$
\mathbf{S}(u,v) = \sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u)\,N_{j,q}(v)\,\mathbf{P}_{i,j}
$$

Here $p$ and $q$ are the orders in the $u$ and $v$ directions respectively, and $\mathbf{P}_{i,j}$ is the **2D control net** of size $(n+1) \times (m+1)$.

### Step 5 — Apply the rational (NURBS) weighting for true conic and quadric reproduction.

$$
\mathbf{S}(u,v) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u)\,N_{j,q}(v)\,w_{i,j}\,\mathbf{P}_{i,j}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u)\,N_{j,q}(v)\,w_{i,j}}
$$

**Why we need the rational form:** A non-rational B-spline **cannot** exactly represent a circle, sphere, cylinder, or cone. With $w_{i,j}$ as weights, the equation is in **homogeneous coordinates** and exact quadric reproduction becomes possible — essential for the spherical joints of a robot arm or the fillet arcs of a mouse.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection for Each Specimen

| Geometry | Dominant Surface Type | Primary CAD Tool | Secondary Tools | Quality Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Helmet** | Doubly-curved dome (ellipsoidal) | Loft | Revolve, Fillet, Knit | Zebra analysis for paint-reflect |
| **Mouse** | Ergonomic doubly-curved freeform | Boundary Surface | Loft, Variable Fillet, Shell, Mirror | Curvature comb visualisation |
| **Robot Arm Link** | Revolved + Extruded mixed | Revolve + Sweep | Blend, Trim, Boolean Cut | Deviation check on shoulder fillet |
| **Robot Joint** | Spherical / toroidal | Sweep along arc | Boundary, Fillet | G2 check at blend entry/exit |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration — Helmet Loft Patch Count

A helmet is lofted through $k = 9$ cross-sections. Each cross-section is a closed spline with $n_c = 16$ control points. The helmet is modelled symmetrically, so only half is lofted and then mirrored.

**Total raw control points before mirroring:**

$$
N_{\text{raw}} = k \times n_c = 9 \times 16 = 144
$$

**After mirroring (counting symmetry only once for the seam):**

$$
N_{\text{ctrl}} = 2 \times 144 - n_c = 288 - 16 = 272
$$

**Knot vector length** in $u$ direction:

$$
L_u = N_{\text{raw}} + p + 1 = 144 + 3 + 1 = 148
$$

using cubic order $p = 3$.

**Maximum patch deviation** for an acceptable Class-B helmet (industrial safety):

$$
R_{\text{dev}} \le 0.10\ \text{mm}
$$

If the zebra-stripe analysis reveals a sharp transition at the crown, the surface must be *re-blended* with a larger fillet radius and the loft sections re-distributed using the **Curvature-Continuous Loft** option in CATIA or the **Global Loft** in SolidWorks.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration — Mouse Shell Thickness and Mass Estimate

A mouse body has surface area (measured by the CAD tool) $A_s = 0.0185\ \text{m}^2$. Shell thickness $t = 1.5\ \text{mm}$. Material: ABS plastic, density $\rho = 1050\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

$$
V_{\text{shell}} = A_s \times t = 0.0185 \times 0.0015 = 2.775 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
m_{\text{shell}} = \rho \times V_{\text{shell}} = 1050 \times 2.775 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0291\ \text{kg} \approx 29.1\ \text{g}
$$

This lies comfortably in the typical 25 – 35 g range for a wireless mouse body, validating the surface model.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Illustration — Robot Arm Reach Using Surface Bounding Box

For a 6-DOF robot arm, each link $i$ has a surface-defined bounding cylinder of length $L_i$ and average radius $r_i$. Total reach envelope:

$$
R_{\text{max}} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} L_i + r_{\text{end-effector}}
$$

For a typical collaborative robot (cobot) with links $L_1 = 0.30\ \text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.30\ \text{m}$, $L_3 = 0.25\ \texttext{m}$, $L_4 = 0.08\ \text{m}$, $L_5 = 0.08\ \text{m}$, $L_6 = 0.05\ \text{m}$ and $r_{\text{ee}} = 0.04\ \text{m}$:

$$
R_{\text{max}} = 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.08 + 0.08 + 0.05 + 0.04 = 1.10\ \text{m}
$$

This matches the catalogue value of a 1.10 m reach cobot (UR3e ≈ 0.50 m; UR5e ≈ 0.85 m; UR10e ≈ 1.30 m) and confirms that the surface model is geometrically consistent.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> **"Define a NURBS surface. List any four advantages of NURBS over a pure Bezier surface."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline) surface** is a parametric surface defined as the ratio of two B-spline tensor-product polynomials in the parameters $u$ and $v$, with each control point carrying an associated weight $w_{i,j}$:

$$
\mathbf{S}(u,v) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u)\,N_{j,q}(v)\,w_{i,j}\,\mathbf{P}_{i,j}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u)\,N_{j,q}(v)\,w_{i,j}}
$$

**Advantages over a Bezier surface:**

1. **Local control** — moving a single control point affects only a localised neighbourhood of the surface, not the entire patch as in Bezier.
2. **Exact quadric representation** — circles, spheres, cylinders and cones are reproduced exactly using weights; Bezier can only approximate them.
3. **Variable knot spacing** — the *non-uniform* knot vector permits dense control in regions of high curvature and sparse control in flat regions, reducing file size.
4. **Stable evaluation** — the de Boor algorithm guarantees numerical stability for high-order surfaces, allowing complex robot-arm and helmet geometry without oscillation artefacts.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> **"What is meant by surface continuity? Distinguish between G1 and G2 continuity with a sketch."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Surface continuity** is a measure of how smoothly two adjacent surface patches join at a common edge. The geometric continuity is classified as:

- **G1 (Tangential continuity):** the tangent vectors of the two surfaces are **parallel** at the common edge, i.e. $\mathbf{T}_1 = k\,\mathbf{T}_2$ for some positive scalar $k$. Highlights on a painted surface appear continuous, but a curvature comb reveals a *kink* in the second derivative.

- **G2 (Curvature continuity):** in addition to G1, the **curvatures** of the two surfaces are equal at the edge, i.e. $\kappa_1 = \kappa_2$. This is the minimum requirement for an automotive *Class-A* finish where reflections must not show any visible discontinuity.

**Sketch description (describe in words for a written exam):** Draw two patches A and B meeting at edge E. Mark the tangent vector $\mathbf{T}$ on each side of the edge — for G1 they are parallel, for G2 the curvature circles on each side of the edge have the **same radius and centre location**, giving a single osculating circle spanning both patches.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> **(a) With the help of a neat step-by-step procedure, explain how you would model the surface of a protective helmet using surface modelling tools in a CAD package. List the analyses you would perform before thickening into a solid. (7 marks)**

> **(b) Explain with sketches the five different methods of generating a freeform surface in CAD software. For each, state one engineering example. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Helmet Modelling Procedure

**Step 1 — Reference Import.** Import front, side and top orthographic reference images of an IS:2925-compliant industrial helmet onto the three default planes of the CAD package at correct scale.

**Step 2 — Centreline Sketch.** On the *Top* plane, draw the crown centreline as a 2D spline starting from the front rim, peaking at the crown, and trailing down to the rear rim. This is the *guide curve* for the loft.

**Step 3 — Cross-Section Sketches.** On the *Front* plane, create 9 cross-section ellipses from the brow band (large $a = 90\ \text{mm}$, $b = 110\ \text{mm}$) to the crown (small $a = 35\ \text{mm}$, $b = 25\ \text{mm}$). Use the *Convert Entities* command to lock the cross-sections to the reference silhouette.

**Step 4 — Loft.** Invoke the **Loft** command. Select all 9 cross-sections and the centreline as the centreline constraint. Set the *Start/End Tangency* to *Normal to Profile* to prevent end-loops.

**Step 5 — Visor Opening.** Create a closed sketch of the visor perimeter on the helmet surface and use *Trim Surface* to cut the opening.

**Step 6 — Ear Cover Loft.** Repeat Steps 3–4 to build a separate patch over each ear region and *Knit* it to the main body.

**Step 7 — Edge Fillets.** Apply a *Constant Radius Fillet* of $R = 8\ \text{mm}$ at the rim and a *Variable Radius Fillet* of $R_1 = 15\ \text{mm}$ to $R_2 = 5\ \text{mm}$ at the chin-strap mounts.

**Step 8 — Surface Analysis before Solid Conversion.**

- **Zebra analysis** to check reflection continuity.
- **Curvature comb** to confirm G2 transitions at every fillet.
- **Deviation analysis** between the original reference image and the projected silhouette of the model.

**Step 9 — Knit and Thicken.** Use the *Knit Surface* tool to merge all patches into one watertight body. Apply *Thicken* outward by $t = 3\ \text{mm}$.

**Step 10 — Drafting Output.** Generate an ISO-view drawing with GD&T (General Tolerance $\pm 0.2\ \text{mm}$, Surface Roughness Ra $1.6\ \mu\text{m}$) for manufacturing.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Five Methods of Surface Generation

| # | Method | Procedure (one-liner) | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Extrude** | A 2D profile is pushed linearly along a vector | Flat panel, name plate, gasket |
| 2 | **Revolve** | A 2D profile is rotated about an axis | Brake drum, lampshade, robot shoulder ball |
| 3 | **Sweep** | A 2D profile is moved along a 3D path | Helmet visor, cable channel, pipe bend |
| 4 | **Loft** | A surface is generated through a series of intermediate cross-sections | Helmet dome, mouse body, bottle |
| 5 | **Boundary** | A surface is generated through 4 (or 3) boundary curves with optional internal spines | Aircraft wing skin, ergonomic car dashboard |

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> **(a) Describe in detail the procedure to model the ergonomic surface of a computer mouse using lofting and blending operations. State the design intent parameters. (7 marks)**

> **(b) Derive the B-spline basis function using the Cox–de Boor recursion formula starting from the order-0 step function. Show the specialisation to the cubic case $p = 3$ and state two engineering applications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Computer Mouse Surface Modelling

**Step 1 — Reference Geometry.** Take ergonomic reference dimensions from a real mouse: length $L = 120\ \text{mm}$, width $W = 68\ \text{mm}$, palm-rest height $H_p = 38\ \text{mm}$, thumb-rest valley depth $d_t = 6\ \text{mm}$.

**Step 2 — Side Profile Sketch.** On the *Right* plane, sketch the side profile of the mouse using a smooth spline: starts at $z = 18\ \text{mm}$ at the front, peaks at $z = 38\ \text{mm}$ under the palm knuckle, and trails to $z = 25\ \text{mm}$ at the rear.

**Step 3 — Front Profile Sketch.** On the *Front* plane, sketch the mouse cross-section near the button divider — a shallow V-shape with the button valleys dropping by $5\ \text{mm}$ from the central peak.

**Step 4 — Symmetry Profile Sketch.** On the *Top* plane, sketch the **plan-view outline** of the mouse — a rounded teardrop shape, $120 \times 68\ \text{mm}$ — and use *Project* to drop it onto the sketch plane of the future surface.

**Step 5 — Boundary Surface.** Use the *Boundary Surface* command. Specify the 4 boundary curves: side profile, top plan-view, front profile, and the bottom (flat) curve. Set the continuity to *Tangent* in both $u$ and $v$ directions to enforce G1 across all 4 edges.

**Step 6 — Thumb-Rest Variable Fillet.** On the left flank of the mouse, create a closed loop representing the thumb-rest depression. Apply a *Variable Radius Fillet* with start radius $R_1 = 12\ \text{mm}$ and end radius $R_2 = 4\ \text{mm}$ to create a smooth organic hollow.

**Step 7 — Scroll-Wheel Cutout.** Sketch a $24\ \text{mm} \times 18\ \text{mm}$ rounded rectangle on the top surface and *Trim Surface* to carve the slot.

**Step 8 — Mirror.** Mirror the half-body across the central symmetry plane to obtain the full mouse. Set the merge tolerance to $0.01\ \text{mm}$ to ensure watertight joining.

**Step 9 — Knit and Shell.** Knit into one patch. Apply *Shell* to give a uniform $1.5\ \text{mm}$ wall thickness.

**Step 10 — Design Intent Parameters (KTU Examiner's Pet Topic).**

- **Wall thickness:** $t = 1.5\ \text{mm}$ ± $0.1\ \text{mm}$ (must be uniform to prevent sink marks in injection moulding).
- **Draft angle:** $\ge 1°$ on all faces normal to the mould-pull direction.
- **Rib thickness:** $0.6\,t = 0.9\ \text{mm}$ to avoid warpage.
- **Boss-to-wall gap:** $\ge 2\,t$ to prevent sink marks.
- **Fillet radius** at all sharp edges: minimum $R = 0.5\ \text{mm}$ to reduce mould stress.

**Step 11 — Quality Analysis.** Zebra + Curvature comb at the thumb-rest blend, then *Draft Analysis* to verify all surfaces release from the mould.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Cox–de Boor Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the order-0 basis as a step function.**

$$
N_{i,0}(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & u_i \le u < u_{i+1} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This means that at order 0, the basis is *active* only over one knot span. The sum $\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,0}(u) = 1$ at every $u$ — this is the **partition of unity**.

**Step 2 — Derive the order-1 (linear) basis by averaging two adjacent order-0 bases with linear weights.**

For $u \in [u_i,\, u_{i+1}]$, $N_{i,0} = 1$ and $N_{i-1,0} = 1$ for the adjacent interval. The blend:

$$
N_{i,1}(u) = \frac{u_{i+1} - u}{u_{i+1} - u_i}\,N_{i,0}(u) + \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+2} - u_i}\,N_{i+1,0}(u)
$$

The first weight is 1 at $u = u_i$ and 0 at $u = u_{i+1}$, the second is the mirror — this guarantees $N_{i,1}(u_i) = 0$, $N_{i,1}(u_{i+1}) = 1$, $N_{i,1}(u_{i+2}) = 0$ — a triangular hat function.

**Step 3 — Generalise by induction to order $p$.**

$$
N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+p} - u_i}\,N_{i,p-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+p+1} - u}{u_{i+p+1} - u_{i+1}}\,N_{i+1,p-1}(u)
$$

The two parent functions $N_{i,p-1}$ and $N_{i+1,p-1}$ are blended with weights that sum to 1 — preserving partition of unity at every order.

**Step 4 — Specialise to the cubic case $p = 3$.**

Substitute $p = 3$ three times in the recursion. For a uniform knot vector $u_{i+1} - u_i = h$:

$$
N_{i,3}(u) = \frac{1}{6h^3} \begin{cases} (-u^3 + 3u^2h - 3uh^2 + h^3), & u \in [u_i, u_{i+1}] \\ (4u^3 - 15u^2h + 18uh^2 - 7h^3), & u \in [u_{i+1}, u_{i+2}] \\ (-4u^3 + 21u^2h - 36uh^2 + 20h^3), & u \in [u_{i+2}, u_{i+3}] \\ (u^3 - 6u^2h + 12uh^2 - 8h^3), & u \in [u_{i+3}, u_{i+4}] \end{cases}
$$

This is the familiar **B-spline cubic** that underlies every modern CAD package's default curve.

**Step 5 — Two engineering applications of the cubic B-spline.**

1. **Cubic NURBS helmet and mouse skins** — default curve type in SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, NX, Fusion 360; provides $C^2$ continuity automatically when the knot vector is *open uniform*.
2. **Tool-path smoothing in 5-axis CNC machining** — cubic B-splines are used by post-processors (e.g., Mastercam, NX CAM) to interpolate G01 moves into jerk-limited motion profiles for robot-arm end effectors.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module — KTU 2024 Examiner Observations:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the "Thicken/Shell" step:** students often leave the model as a "surface body" and lose 1 – 2 marks because mass, COG and FEA cannot be performed on a non-solid. Always *Thicken* or *Shell* before submitting.
> 2. **Confusing Position continuity (G0) with Tangent continuity (G1):** examiners explicitly look for the equation $\mathbf{T}_1 = k\,\mathbf{T}_2$ for G1. Writing only "the patches meet smoothly" gets *zero* mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to set design-intent parameters** (draft angle, uniform wall thickness, rib ratio) for plastic parts such as the mouse — this is worth at least **2 marks** in a 7-mark question.
> 4. **Using *Revolve* for a doubly-curved surface:** the helmet and mouse are *doubly* curved and cannot be produced by a single revolve. Examiners deduct marks for this fundamental geometric mistake.
> 5. **No Zebra/Curvature analysis shown:** the question almost always asks for verification, and a screenshot-less answer is marked down by up to **1.5 marks**.
> 6. **Mixing NURBS equation with the polynomial B-spline equation:** students write the *non-rational* B-spline formula and then claim it is NURBS — losing the 1 mark reserved for the weight term $w_{i,j}$.
> 7. **Not using `Knit` to make the body watertight:** the surface-modelling pipeline is incomplete without this step, and downstream FEA will fail with open edges.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Surface modelling represents a body as a **skin** rather than a volume — ideal for **organic, doubly-curved, aerodynamic** shapes.
- [x] The **NURBS** equation has a *rational* form: control points weighted by $w_{i,j}$ in the numerator and a single sum of basis functions in the denominator.
- [x] **Cox–de Boor recursion** generates B-spline bases of any order from the order-0 step function.
- [x] **G0 < G1 < G2 < G3** is the hierarchy of geometric continuities — automotive Class-A needs at least G2.
- [x] **Helmet** → Loft through cross-sections, Knit, Thicken (shell $t \approx 3\ \text{mm}$).
- [x] **Mouse** → Boundary Surface, Variable Fillet for thumb rest, Mirror, Shell ($t = 1.5\ \text{mm}$).
- [x] **Robot arm** → Revolve (joints) + Sweep (links) + Blend (transitions) + Boolean Cut (cable channel).
- [x] **Surface analysis** before thickening: Zebra (reflection), Curvature comb (smoothness), Deviation (tolerance).
- [x] **Design intent** for plastic parts: uniform wall thickness, $\ge 1°$ draft, fillet all sharp edges, rib thickness $0.6\,t$.
- [x] **Final step** is always **Thicken** or **Shell** to convert the surface body into a solid for CAE/CAM.
- [x] The first fundamental form $E, F, G$ comes from the tangent vectors; the second fundamental form $L, M, N$ governs curvature.
- [x] Gaussian curvature $K = (LN - M^2)/(EG - F^2)$; mean curvature $H = (EN - 2FM + GL)/[2(EG - F^2)]$.
- [x] Practical file-exchange formats: **STEP (AP214/AP242)**, **IGES**, **Parasolid (.x_t)**, **STL** (for 3D printing).
- [x] KTU marks are typically awarded in the ratio **Process:Analysis:Final Solid = 4:2:1** — do not skip the analysis step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rapid prototyping of simple CAD models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELLING AND SIMECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Rapid prototyping of simple CAD models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Prototyping (RP)** is a class of **additive manufacturing (AM)** technologies that fabricate physical, three-dimensional objects directly from **computer-aided design (CAD) data** by successively adding material layer-by-layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methods (milling, turning) or formative methods (casting, forging).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** definition for *PCRAL407*, rapid prototyping refers specifically to the **experimental workflow** of converting a simple parametric or solid CAD model (created in CATIA / SolidWorks / Fusion 360 / Creo) into a tangible physical prototype using an **STL file**, followed by **slicing**, **layered fabrication**, and **post-processing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCRAL407 – Module 2 Experiment):** Students are expected to take a simple CAD part (e.g., a flange, bracket, spur gear, or telephone stand), export it as an `.STL` file, slice it using **Cura / Slic3r / IdeaMaker**, estimate print parameters, and physically produce the part on an **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** or **SLA (Stereolithography)** machine. The experimental report must include screenshots of: CAD model → STL export → Slicer view → Printed part.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (High-Yield for KTU Viva)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **CAD** | Computer-Aided Design (parametric solid model) |
| **STL** | Standard Tessellation Language (triangulated mesh) |
| **Slicer** | Software that converts STL into G-code toolpath |
| **G-code** | Numerical control instructions for the 3D printer |
| **FDM** | Fused Deposition Modeling (thermoplastic extrusion) |
| **SLA** | Stereolithography (photopolymer resin + UV laser) |
| **Layer Height** | Vertical resolution of each deposited layer (typically **0.1 mm to 0.3 mm**) |
| **Infill** | Internal density percentage (typically **15% – 25%**) |
| **Build Volume** | Maximum printable XYZ envelope (e.g., **220 × 220 × 250 mm**) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cake That Builds Itself"

Imagine baking a **wedding cake**, but instead of carving a single large cake into shape, you **slice a 3D digital design into hundreds of thin horizontal cross-sections** and stack them one on top of the other using icing (material). Each layer is only **0.1 – 0.2 mm thick**, and the printer lays them down like a baker piping frosting, slowly building a solid 3D object from the bottom up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A 3D printer is essentially a **2D printer that prints layer after layer, each one a thin horizontal slice of your 3D CAD model.** You draw it on a computer (CAD), save the geometry as a faceted mesh (STL), tell the slicer how thick each layer should be and how dense the inside should be (infill), and the printer does the rest.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard Layer Thickness (FDM):** **0.1 mm** (high quality), **0.2 mm** (standard), **0.3 mm** (draft)
- **Nozzle Diameter:** Typically **0.4 mm**
- **Standard Infill Density:** **20 %** (decorative) to **100 %** (functional)
- **STL Tolerance (Chordal Deviation):** **0.01 mm** to **0.05 mm**
- **Build Platform Temperature:** **60 °C** (for ABS) to **60 °C – 110 °C** (for PLA)
- **Extruder Temperature:** **190 °C – 220 °C** (PLA), **230 °C – 260 °C** (ABS)
- **Volumetric Flow Rate:** Typical range **8 – 12 mm³/s**

---

## 1.5 Why This Experiment Matters (KTU Outcome Mapping)

| Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Mapped Skill |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** – Apply CAD tools to develop mechanical models | Apply / Create | Build the CAD model in 3D |
| **CO4** – Simulate and fabricate components | Apply | Operate slicer & printer |
| **CO5** – Interpret RP process parameters | Analyze | Tune infill, layer height, orientation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rapid Prototyping Workflow (5-Stage Pipeline)

The complete RP chain for a simple CAD model is a **sequential, irreversible process**. A student must understand each stage before performing the experiment.

### Stage 1 — CAD Model Creation
- Create a parametric solid model in **SolidWorks / CATIA / Fusion 360 / Creo Parametric**.
- Apply correct **dimensional constraints**, **datum planes**, and **material density** (e.g., PLA = **1.24 g/cm³**).
- Save the file in native CAD format (`.sldprt`, `.CATPart`, `.f3d`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this stage matters:** Errors at the CAD stage propagate through every downstream stage. A non-manifold or inverted-normal solid will produce a corrupt STL.

### Stage 2 — STL Export (Tessellation)
- CAD model → **triangulated surface mesh** (`.STL` format).
- Two STL variants: **ASCII STL** (verbose, large) and **Binary STL** (compact, preferred).
- The user defines a **chordal deviation** (max deviation between true surface and faceted approximation), typically **0.01 mm – 0.05 mm**.

### Stage 3 — Slicing
- STL is loaded into a **slicer** (Cura, Slic3r, PrusaSlicer, IdeaMaker).
- The slicer performs **vector slicing**: it intersects the mesh with horizontal planes spaced by the **layer height** $h$.
- The slicer generates **G-code**: a sequential list of (X, Y, Z, E, F) moves for the printer.
- Slicer also generates the **perimeters (shells)**, **top/bottom solid layers**, and the **infill pattern** (lines, grid, gyroid, honeycomb).

### Stage 4 — 3D Printing / Additive Fabrication
- The printer reads the G-code line by line.
- For **FDM**: filament is fed into a heated nozzle, melted, and extruded onto the build plate.
- For **SLA**: a UV laser cures photopolymer resin point-by-point in each layer.
- For **SLS**: a laser sinters powdered polymer (nylon, TPU) layer by layer.

### Stage 5 — Post-Processing
- **Part removal** from the build plate.
- **Support removal** (FDM/SLA).
- **Curing** (SLA, 5–10 min under UV).
- **Sanding, painting, vapor smoothing** (optional, cosmetic).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following formulas are the most frequently asked numerical/numerical-conceptual questions in KTU lab exams and viva. Memorize the units and what each variable represents.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{part}} = L \times W \times H$ | Bounding box volume (over-estimate) | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| 2 | $V_{\text{infill}} = V_{\text{shell}} + \rho \cdot (V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}})$ | Total filament volume | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| 3 | $m_{\text{filament}} = V_{\text{infill}} \times \rho_{\text{material}}$ | Mass of filament used | grams |
| 4 | $t_{\text{print}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{layers}} \times A_{\text{per\_layer}}}{Q}$ | Estimated print time | minutes / hours |
| 5 | $N_{\text{layers}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{part}}}{h_{\text{layer}}}$ | Number of layers | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{\text{material}} = m_{\text{filament}} \times c_{\text{per\_g}}$ | Material cost | ₹ / $ |
| 7 | $A_{\text{shell}} = 2 \cdot (LW + LH + WH) \cdot n_{\text{perimeters}}$ | Outer wall surface area | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{tensile}} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Functional part strength check | MPa |
| 9 | $R_a = f(h_{\text{layer}})$ | Surface roughness depends on layer height | μm |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{part}}} \times 100\%$ | Material utilization efficiency | % |

**Where:**
- $L, W, H$ = length, width, height of the bounding box
- $\rho$ = infill density (e.g., 0.20 for 20 %)
- $h_{\text{layer}}$ = layer thickness (0.1–0.3 mm)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (mm³/s)
- $n_{\text{perimeters}}$ = number of outer wall lines (typically 2 or 3)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Jigs, fixtures, ergonomic concept models |
| **Medical** | Patient-specific anatomical models, surgical guides |
| **Aerospace** | Topology-optimized lightweight brackets |
| **Tooling** | Injection mold inserts with conformal cooling |
| **Education** | Visual-tactile learning aids, KTU lab coursework |
| **Foundry** | Investment casting patterns (e.g., Impeller blades) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Rapid prototyping is the **bridge between virtual CAD and physical testing**. The lab experiment is graded not just on getting a print, but on the student's ability to **justify layer height, infill, orientation, and support placement** in the report.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Filament Mass Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A student prints a rectangular **phone stand** with bounding-box dimensions $L = 120\,\text{mm}$, $W = 80\,\text{mm}$, $H = 100\,\text{mm}$. The print uses **3 perimeter walls**, **20 % infill**, **layer height** $h = 0.2\,\text{mm}$, and **PLA filament** with density $\rho_{\text{PLA}} = 1.24\,\text{g/cm}^3$. Calculate: (a) the bounding-box volume, (b) the number of layers, (c) the shell volume, and (d) the mass of PLA consumed.

### Step 1 — Bounding Box Volume

$$V_{\text{part}} = L \times W \times H$$

Substituting the values:

$$V_{\text{part}} = 120\,\text{mm} \times 80\,\text{mm} \times 100\,\text{mm}$$

$$V_{\text{part}} = 960{,}000\,\text{mm}^3$$

Converting to cm³ (since density is in g/cm³):

$$V_{\text{part}} = 960{,}000\,\text{mm}^3 \times \frac{1\,\text{cm}^3}{1000\,\text{mm}^3} = 960\,\text{cm}^3$$

### Step 2 — Number of Layers

$$N_{\text{layers}} = \frac{H_{\text{part}}}{h_{\text{layer}}}$$

$$N_{\text{layers}} = \frac{100\,\text{mm}}{0.2\,\text{mm}} = 500\,\text{layers}$$

### Step 3 — Shell Volume (Outer Perimeters)

The shell thickness per perimeter is approximately equal to the nozzle diameter $d_n = 0.4\,\text{mm}$. With $n = 3$ perimeters, total shell thickness is:

$$t_{\text{shell}} = n \times d_n = 3 \times 0.4\,\text{mm} = 1.2\,\text{mm}$$

Outer surface area:

$$A_{\text{shell}} = 2 \cdot (LW + LH + WH)$$

$$A_{\text{shell}} = 2 \cdot (120 \times 80 + 120 \times 100 + 80 \times 100)$$

$$A_{\text{shell}} = 2 \cdot (9600 + 12000 + 8000) = 2 \cdot 29600 = 59{,}200\,\text{mm}^2$$

Shell volume:

$$V_{\text{shell}} = A_{\text{shell}} \times t_{\text{shell}} = 59{,}200 \times 1.2 = 71{,}040\,\text{mm}^3 = 71.04\,\text{cm}^3$$

### Step 4 — Infill Region Volume

$$V_{\text{infill\_region}} = V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}}$$

$$V_{\text{infill\_region}} = 960 - 71.04 = 888.96\,\text{cm}^3$$

Actual infill volume (20 % density):

$$V_{\text{infill}} = 0.20 \times 888.96 = 177.79\,\text{cm}^3$$

### Step 5 — Total Material Volume & Mass

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{shell}} + V_{\text{infill}} = 71.04 + 177.79 = 248.83\,\text{cm}^3$$

$$m_{\text{PLA}} = V_{\text{total}} \times \rho_{\text{PLA}} = 248.83 \times 1.24$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{PLA}} \approx 308.55\,\text{g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** A common student error is to use $V_{\text{part}}$ directly with density. Always subtract the shell and apply the infill percentage to the **remaining volume only**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Print Time Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Continuing Problem 1, the slicer reports an **average per-layer extrusion area** $A_{\text{per\_layer}} = 12\,\text{mm}^2$ (effective cross-section of infill + shell on each layer). The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 8\,\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$. Estimate the **print time** in minutes.

### Step 1 — Volume per Layer

$$V_{\text{layer}} = A_{\text{per\_layer}} \times h_{\text{layer}} = 12 \times 0.2 = 2.4\,\text{mm}^3$$

### Step 2 — Total Extrusion Volume (already calculated in Problem 1)

$$V_{\text{total}} = 248.83\,\text{cm}^3 = 248{,}830\,\text{mm}^3$$

### Step 3 — Total Print Time (Seconds)

$$T_{\text{sec}} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{Q} = \frac{248{,}830}{8} = 31{,}103.75\,\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Minutes

$$T_{\text{min}} = \frac{31{,}103.75}{60}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{min}} \approx 518.4\,\text{min} \approx 8.64\,\text{hours}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world note:** The actual print time will be longer because the formula above excludes **travel moves**, **acceleration/deceleration**, and **retraction**. Slicer software typically reports 10 % – 20 % longer than this theoretical estimate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Part Orientation Trade-offs

| Orientation | Surface Finish (Z+) | Strength (Z axis) | Support Needed? | Print Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat on bed** (XY plane) | Excellent (top surface smooth) | Weak (delamination risk) | Minimal / None | Fast |
| **On its side** (XZ plane) | Good (sides show stair-stepping) | Strong in Z | Moderate | Medium |
| **Tilted 45°** | Poor (visible steps) | Anisotropic | High | Slow |
| **Vertical (Z up)** | Poor (staircase effect) | Strongest in Z | High | Slowest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favorite:** "Why do we prefer the flat orientation?" — Because the **largest face** has the **best surface finish** and **no support material** is required, saving post-processing time.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Table — Common RP Technologies

| Parameter | FDM | SLA | SLS | PolyJet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Thermoplastic (PLA, ABS) | Photopolymer resin | Nylon powder | Photopolymer resin |
| **Layer Height** | 0.1–0.3 mm | 0.025–0.1 mm | 0.08–0.15 mm | 0.016–0.028 mm |
| **Tolerance** | ±0.2 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.3 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Support** | Required for overhangs > 45° | Always required | Not required | Required |
| **Cost (per cm³)** | Low | Medium | High | Very high |
| **Best For (KTU Lab)** | Functional parts | Aesthetic / visual parts | Functional nylon | Multi-material / soft |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End RP Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Engineering Idea] --> B[Create 3D CAD Model in SolidWorks or CATIA]
    B --> C[Apply Constraints and Dimensions]
    C --> D[Export as STL File]
    D --> E[Choose Chordal Deviation 0.01 to 0.05 mm]
    E --> F[Import STL into Slicer Software]
    F --> G[Set Layer Height and Infill Density]
    G --> H[Generate G-code Toolpath]
    H --> I[Transfer G-code to 3D Printer]
    I --> J[Printer Builds Part Layer by Layer]
    J --> K[Remove Part from Build Plate]
    K --> L[Remove Supports and Post-process]
    L --> M[Final Physical Prototype Ready]
    M --> N[End: Compare with Original CAD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each rectangular node represents a **process stage**. Arrows indicate the **unidirectional flow** of data and material. The dashed transition from D to F implies the STL file is the *bridge* between CAD and manufacturing.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — FDM Printer Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ControlUnit["Control Unit"]
        MCU[Microcontroller Board]
        FW[Firmware Marlin]
    end

    subgraph MotionStage["Motion Stage"]
        SX[Stepper X axis]
        SY[Stepper Y axis]
        SZ[Stepper Z axis]
    end

    subgraph ExtrusionUnit["Extrusion Unit"]
        EXT[Extruder Motor]
        HTR[Heated Nozzle 200 C]
        FIL[PLA Filament 1.75 mm]
    end

    subgraph BuildPlatform["Build Platform"]
        BP[Heated Bed 60 C]
        BPF[Build Plate Surface]
    end

    MCU --> SX
    MCU --> SY
    MCU --> SZ
    MCU --> EXT
    FIL --> EXT
    EXT --> HTR
    HTR --> BPF
    BP --> BPF
    FW --> MCU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Mapping:** The **Control Unit** executes G-code instructions. The **Motion Stage** positions the nozzle in X, Y, Z space. The **Extrusion Unit** melts and deposits thermoplastic. The **Build Platform** provides a stable, temperature-controlled surface for layer adhesion.

---

## 4.3 Layered Manufacturing Schematic (Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Base 0.0 to 0.2 mm] --> L2[Layer 2: 0.2 to 0.4 mm]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: 0.4 to 0.6 mm]
    L3 --> L4[Layer N: H minus 0.2 to H mm]
    L4 --> FINAL[Final Part: H mm Tall]

    subgraph CrossSection["Single Layer Cross Section"]
        SH1[Outer Wall Perimeter 0.4 mm]
        INF[Internal Infill Grid 20 percent]
        SH1 --- INF
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The left stack represents the **vertical build sequence** from layer 1 (deposited first) to the top layer. The right block represents a **single horizontal slice**, showing the perimeter wall surrounding a sparse internal infill.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flow — When to Add Supports

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does the part have overhangs greater than 45 degrees from vertical?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Are the overhangs longer than 5 mm?}
    Q1 -->|No| NOSUPP[No Supports Needed]
    Q2 -->|Yes| ADDSUPP[Add Tree or Linear Supports]
    Q2 -->|No| OPTIONAL[Supports Optional]
    ADDSUPP --> ORIENT{Reorient Part Possible?}
    ORIENT -->|Yes| REORIENT[Reorient to Avoid Supports]
    ORIENT -->|No| KEEPSUPP[Keep Supports and Print]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** The **45° rule** is a golden rule in FDM. Overhangs shallower than 45° from vertical print successfully without support; steeper overhangs sag or fail. This is one of the most common viva questions in KTU lab exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A contains 2–3 mark direct questions. Answers should be 3–5 lines with a clear definition and one supporting point.

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define rapid prototyping. List any three rapid prototyping processes.**

**Model Answer:**
Rapid prototyping (RP) is a group of **additive manufacturing techniques** used to fabricate a physical model directly from **3D CAD data** by depositing material **layer-by-layer**, as opposed to traditional subtractive methods.
Three common RP processes are:
1. **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** — extrudes thermoplastic filament.
2. **SLA (Stereolithography)** — cures photopolymer resin using a UV laser.
3. **SLS (Selective Laser Sintering)** — sinters powdered material using a laser.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is an STL file? Why is it used in rapid prototyping?**

**Model Answer:**
**STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** is a file format that represents a 3D CAD model as a collection of **triangular facets** approximating the original surfaces.
It is used in RP because:
- It is the **universal input format** accepted by virtually all slicer software.
- It is **simple, geometry-only** (no color, material, or CAD history).
- It can be quickly **sliced into 2D layers** for layer-by-layer fabrication.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and must contain two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Internal choice is provided at the module level. Students must answer only **one** of the two choices (A or B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step workflow of rapid prototyping** starting from a 3D CAD model to the final physical part. Mention the role of the **slicer** and **G-code** in the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simple CAD part has bounding-box dimensions $L = 100\,\text{mm}$, $W = 60\,\text{mm}$, $H = 40\,\text{mm}$. The slicer is configured with **layer height** $h = 0.2\,\text{mm}$, **2 perimeter walls** with nozzle diameter $d_n = 0.4\,\text{mm}$, and **infill density** = 25 %. The filament material is **ABS** with density $\rho_{\text{ABS}} = 1.04\,\text{g/cm}^3$. Calculate:
- (i) Number of layers
- (ii) Shell volume
- (iii) Total mass of ABS consumed **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

The RP workflow consists of **five sequential stages**:

1. **CAD Model Creation:** A 3D parametric model is created in **SolidWorks / CATIA / Fusion 360** with proper constraints.
2. **STL Export:** The CAD model is converted into a **triangulated mesh (.STL)**. The chordal deviation is set to **0.01 mm – 0.05 mm**.
3. **Slicing:** The STL is loaded into a **slicer** (e.g., Cura). The slicer intersects the mesh with horizontal planes at a spacing equal to the **layer height** $h$. It generates **perimeters, infill patterns, top/bottom layers, and supports**, and outputs a **G-code file**.
4. **3D Printing:** The G-code is sent to the printer. In **FDM**, a heated nozzle (190 °C – 260 °C) extrudes molten thermoplastic onto a heated bed (60 °C – 110 °C).
5. **Post-Processing:** The part is removed from the bed; supports are detached; the surface is sanded or painted if needed.

**Role of Slicer:** Acts as the **translator** between 3D geometry and machine motion — converts meshes into per-layer toolpaths.
**Role of G-code:** It is the **machine language** of the 3D printer, containing X, Y, Z, E (extruder) and F (feed rate) instructions executed line by line.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
$L = 100\,\text{mm}$, $W = 60\,\text{mm}$, $H = 40\,\text{mm}$, $h = 0.2\,\text{mm}$, $n = 2$, $d_n = 0.4\,\text{mm}$, $\rho = 25\,\% = 0.25$, $\rho_{\text{ABS}} = 1.04\,\text{g/cm}^3$.

**(i) Number of Layers:**

$$N_{\text{layers}} = \frac{H}{h} = \frac{40}{0.2} = 200\,\text{layers}$$

**(ii) Shell Volume:**

$$t_{\text{shell}} = n \times d_n = 2 \times 0.4 = 0.8\,\text{mm}$$

$$A_{\text{shell}} = 2 \cdot (LW + LH + WH) = 2 \cdot (6000 + 4000 + 2400) = 24{,}800\,\text{mm}^2$$

$$V_{\text{shell}} = A_{\text{shell}} \times t_{\text{shell}} = 24{,}800 \times 0.8 = 19{,}840\,\text{mm}^3 = 19.84\,\text{cm}^3$$

**Bounding box volume:**

$$V_{\text{part}} = 100 \times 60 \times 40 = 240{,}000\,\text{mm}^3 = 240\,\text{cm}^3$$

**Infill region volume:**

$$V_{\text{infill\_region}} = 240 - 19.84 = 220.16\,\text{cm}^3$$

**Infill volume (25 %):**

$$V_{\text{infill}} = 0.25 \times 220.16 = 55.04\,\text{cm}^3$$

**(iii) Total Mass of ABS:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{shell}} + V_{\text{infill}} = 19.84 + 55.04 = 74.88\,\text{cm}^3$$

$$m_{\text{ABS}} = 74.88 \times 1.04$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{ABS}} \approx 77.88\,\text{g}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Compare the **FDM, SLA, and SLS** rapid prototyping processes based on **principle of operation, material used, surface finish, accuracy, and typical applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of part orientation and support structures** in FDM printing. With a diagram, illustrate the **45° overhang rule** and discuss how it influences the decision to add supports. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Parameter | FDM | SLA | SLS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Extrudes melted thermoplastic through a heated nozzle | Cures photopolymer resin with a UV laser | Sinters nylon powder with a CO₂ laser |
| **Material** | PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU | Liquid photopolymer resin | Nylon (PA12), TPU powder |
| **Layer Height** | 0.1 – 0.3 mm | 0.025 – 0.1 mm | 0.08 – 0.15 mm |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate, visible layer lines | Excellent, near-injection-molded | Good, slightly grainy |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.2 mm | ±0.05 – 0.1 mm | ±0.2 – 0.3 mm |
| **Supports** | Required for overhangs > 45° | Always required | Not required (unsupported powder bed) |
| **Typical Application** | Functional prototypes, jigs, brackets | Aesthetic models, jewelry, dental | Functional nylon parts, lattice structures |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

**Conclusion:** FDM is the **most accessible** technology (used in KTU labs); SLA produces the **smoothest surface**; SLS produces the **strongest functional parts** without supports.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Part Orientation in FDM:**
The orientation of a part on the build plate has a direct effect on:
- **Surface quality** (top face vs side faces)
- **Mechanical strength** (anisotropy between X/Y and Z directions)
- **Support requirements** (overhangs)
- **Print time and material consumption**

**The 45° Overhang Rule:**
In FDM, any surface that **overhangs more than 45° from the vertical** (i.e., the angle between the overhang and the vertical build direction is greater than 45°) will **sag, droop, or collapse** because there is no underlying material to support the freshly extruded plastic.

**Diagram (schematic side view):**

```
        Safe overhang (< 45°)        Unsafe overhang (> 45°)
        ────────────────────          ────────────────────
        |  /                          |       /
        | /                           |      /
        |/                            |     /
       ────  Build plate            ──── Build plate
        (NO support needed)            (SUPPORT needed)
```

**Influence on Supports:**
- If the part contains overhangs **shallower than 45°**, no supports are required — the printer successfully bridges the gap.
- If overhangs are **steeper than 45°** and span **more than ~5 mm**, **tree supports or linear supports** must be generated by the slicer.
- Alternatively, the part may be **re-oriented** to convert steep overhangs into shallow ones, eliminating the need for supports entirely and improving the surface finish on visible faces.

**Engineering Heuristic:** Always orient the part so its **largest flat face** lies on the build plate, with the **tallest dimension** along the Z axis, to minimize both support usage and stair-stepping artifacts.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Lab Exam / Viva:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm³ to cm³** before multiplying by density. This alone costs 1–2 marks in numericals.
> 2. **Applying infill percentage to the full bounding-box volume** instead of to the *infill region only* (after subtracting the shell).
> 3. **Confusing SLA with SLS** in comparison questions — SLA uses *liquid resin + UV laser*; SLS uses *powder + heat laser*.
> 4. **Drawing the workflow as a cycle** — it is a **linear pipeline**, not a loop.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer.** Always write $m_{\text{PLA}} = 308.55\,\text{g}$ (not just 308.55).
> 6. **Stating "FDM is better than SLA"** in comparison questions — use the word *"trade-off"* and mention the application context.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention supports in the orientation discussion** — it is the most common follow-up viva question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- [ ] **RP = Additive Manufacturing** = Layer-by-layer fabrication from CAD.
- [ ] **Five-stage workflow:** CAD → STL → Slicer → Print → Post-process.
- [ ] **STL format** = triangulated mesh, ASCII or Binary, two file variants.
- [ ] **Slicer** = converts STL into G-code; sets **layer height, infill, perimeters, supports**.
- [ ] **FDM** uses thermoplastic filament and a heated nozzle (**190 °C – 260 °C**).
- [ ] **SLA** uses photopolymer resin + UV laser; produces the **smoothest** surface.
- [ ] **SLS** uses nylon powder; supports are **not required**.
- [ ] **45° overhang rule:** Steeper than 45° → **support required**.
- [ ] **PLA density = 1.24 g/cm³**; **ABS density = 1.04 g/cm³**.
- [ ] **Mass formula:** $m = V_{\text{shell}} \cdot \rho_{\text{material}} + (V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}}) \cdot \rho_{\text{infill}} \cdot \rho_{\text{material}}$.
- [ ] **Number of layers:** $N = H_{\text{part}} / h_{\text{layer}}$.
- [ ] **Shell thickness:** $t_{\text{shell}} = n \times d_n$ (default $d_n = 0.4\,\text{mm}$).
- [ ] **Standard infill for decorative parts = 20 %**; for **functional parts = 50 % – 100 %**.
- [ ] **Print time scales with** $N_{\text{layers}} \times A_{\text{per\_layer}} / Q$ where $Q$ is volumetric flow rate.
- [ ] **Always orient** the part with its **largest flat face on the build plate**.
- [ ] **Remember to report** mass, print time, infill %, layer height, and material in your **KTU lab record**.

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 2 Experiment Notes — Rapid Prototyping of Simple CAD Models**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creation of joints at assembly points and animating their relative motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELLING AND SIMULATION OF MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creation of joints at assembly points and animating their relative motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Computer Aided Modelling and Assembly Design (as per the KTU 2024 Scheme lab syllabus for **PCRAL407**), an **Assembly Joint** (historically referred to as a *Mate* in legacy CAD nomenclature like SolidWorks pre-2015, CATIA V5 *Constraints*, or Creo *Connections*) is a **parametric geometric relationship** imposed between two or more component faces, edges, vertices, or reference geometries within a virtual assembly environment. A joint restricts the relative degrees of freedom (DOF) between components, thereby defining the permissible kinematic behaviour of the mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology:** In modern parametric CAD platforms (SolidWorks 2015+, Fusion 360, Siemens NX), the term "Mate" has been officially replaced by "**Joint**". The KTU lab viva frequently tests the student's ability to distinguish between *Assembly Constraints (Joints)* and *In-Assembly Dimensions*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinematic Definition:** A joint is mathematically represented as a vector function $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2) = \mathbf{0}$, where $\mathbf{q}_1$ and $\mathbf{q}_2$ are the generalized coordinate vectors of the two constrained bodies. The number of scalar equations in this function defines the number of constrained DOF.

### 1.2 Joint Animation — The KTU Lab Definition

**Animation of relative motion** refers to the computational simulation of the time-dependent kinematic state of a constrained assembly by integrating the joint constraints over a prescribed time domain. The position of every component is updated frame-by-frame by solving the constraint Jacobian:

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{b}(t)$$

where $\mathbf{J}$ is the constraint Jacobian matrix, $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is the generalized velocity vector, and $\mathbf{b}(t)$ represents the prescribed motion inputs (from servo motors or kinematic drivers).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Skeleton System**
> 
> Think of a mechanical assembly exactly like a **human skeleton**:
> 
> 1. **Bones** $\rightarrow$ Individual 3D Parts (designed in Part Module of CAD)
> 2. **Ligaments & Tendons** $\rightarrow$ **Joints/Constraints** (limit how bones move relative to each other)
> 3. **Muscle Contractions** $\rightarrow$ **Servo Motors & Motion Drivers** in the animation environment
> 4. **Walking / Running** $\rightarrow$ The resulting **Motion Study / Animation**
> 
> Just as your elbow is a **Revolute Joint** (allows rotation in 1 DOF) and your shoulder is a **Ball Joint** (allows 3 rotational DOF), a CAD assembly uses these same classifications to mathematically define the permitted relative movement.

### 1.4 Critical Classification of Joints (KTU Board Favourite)

The **3D space offers 6 DOF** to every unconstrained body: **3 translations** ($T_x, T_y, T_z$) and **3 rotations** ($R_x, R_y, R_z$). A joint removes some of these:

| Joint Category | CAD Name (Modern) | DOF Allowed | Real-World Example |
|----------------|-------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Rigid Lock** | Lock Mate / Fixed Joint | 0 | Welded bracket |
| **Revolute** | Hinge / Pin Joint | 1 Rotation | Door hinge, engine valve |
| **Slider/Prismatic** | Slider Joint | 1 Translation | Piston in cylinder, drawer |
| **Cylindrical** | Cylinder Joint | 1 Rot + 1 Trans | Screw in threaded hole |
| **Pin (Ball)** | Ball Joint | 3 Rotations | Human shoulder, CV joint |
| **Planar** | Planar Joint | 3 DOF in plane | Book sliding on table |
| **Distance/Angle** | Distance / Angle Mate | Controlled separation | Adjustable bracket |

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Parameters Used in Animation:**
> - **Time Step ($\Delta t$):** Typically **0.01 s to 0.1 s** for smooth animation
> - **Frames Per Second (FPS):** Standard **24 FPS** (cinema) or **30 FPS** (video)
> - **Total Simulation Time ($T$):** Usually **2 to 10 seconds** for lab demonstrations
> - **Servo Motor Range:** Position limits in **millimetres (mm)** or **degrees ($^\circ$)**
> - **Angular Velocity ($\omega$):** Expressed in **RPM** or **rad/s**
> - **DOF in 3D:** Maximum **6 per unconstrained rigid body**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Kinematic Theory

Every rigid body in 3D space has a **6-DOF configuration vector** $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, z, \theta_x, \theta_y, \theta_z]^T$. When two bodies are joined by a constraint, the system eliminates certain DOF. The remaining mobility of the entire mechanism is calculated using the **Grübler's Equation** (also called the Kutzbach criterion in planar mechanisms).

#### 2.1.1 Grübler's Equation for Spatial Mechanisms (3D)

$$M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5} (6-i) \cdot j_i$$

Expanded form for spatial mechanisms:

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

where:
- $M$ = **Mobility** (total DOF of the mechanism)
- $n$ = **Number of links** (including the fixed ground link)
- $j_1$ = Number of joints with **1 DOF** (e.g., Revolute, Prismatic)
- $j_2$ = Number of joints with **2 DOF** (e.g., Cylindrical, Planar in 3D context)
- $j_3$ = Number of joints with **3 DOF** (e.g., Spherical/Ball)
- $j_4$ = Number of joints with **4 DOF**
- $j_5$ = Number of joints with **5 DOF**

#### 2.1.2 Grübler's Equation for Planar Mechanisms (2D)

For mechanisms operating in a single plane:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where:
- $j_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** (1 DOF: Revolute or Prismatic)
- $j_2$ = Number of **higher pairs** (2 DOF: Cam-follower, gear mesh)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Joint Creation in CAD

1. **Identify the kinematic pair** required between two components (e.g., a shaft rotating inside a bearing).
2. **Select reference geometries** on both parts (faces, edges, vertices, planes, or origin points).
3. **Choose the appropriate joint type** from the CAD Joint Manager (e.g., Concentric, Coincident, Revolute).
4. **Apply the joint** — the CAD solver computes the transformation matrix $\mathbf{T}_{4 \times 4}$ that aligns the two parts.
5. **Verify DOF reduction** — the on-screen DOF indicator should drop from 6 toward 0.
6. **Iterate** until the assembly is fully constrained (over-constrained joints throw a warning).

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Joint Type

- **Coincident Joint:** Forces two planar faces to lie in the same infinite plane. Used to "close" parts together like two book covers. *Why?* Removes 2 translations and 2 rotations, leaves 1 rotation + 1 translation.
- **Concentric Joint:** Aligns cylindrical/circular axes. Essential for shafts and holes. *Why?* Removes 2 translations and 2 rotations.
- **Revolute Joint:** Combines Coincident + Concentric, allowing **only 1 rotational DOF** about the shared axis.
- **Slider Joint:** Allows pure translation along a single axis — basis of reciprocating engines.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Grübler (3D) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | $M$ = DOF, $n$ = links, $j_i$ = joint counts | Spatial mechanism analysis |
| Grübler (2D) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $M$ = DOF, $n$ = links | Slider-crank, four-bar |
| Relative Velocity (Revolute) | $\mathbf{v}_{B/A} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{B/A}$ | $\mathbf{v}$ in m/s, $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ in rad/s, $\mathbf{r}$ in m | Pin joint analysis |
| Slider Position (Crank-Slider) | $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ | $x$ in mm, $r, L$ in mm, $\theta$ in rad | Reciprocating engine |
| Angular Velocity Relation | $\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)$ | $N$ = teeth (gears), $\omega$ in rad/s | Gear animation |
| Servo Motor Position (Linear) | $s(t) = s_0 + v \cdot t$ | $s$ in mm, $v$ in mm/s, $t$ in s | Animation input |
| Servo Motor Position (Sine) | $s(t) = s_0 + A\sin(2\pi f t)$ | $A$ = amplitude, $f$ = Hz | Oscillating cam |
| Arc Length (Revolute) | $L = r\theta$ | $L$ in mm, $r$ in mm, $\theta$ in rad | Rotation animation |
| Constraint Jacobian | $\mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{b}(t)$ | $\mathbf{J}$ is $m \times n$ matrix | Solver core equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Joint-based assembly is the digital backbone of **Digital Twins** in Industry 4.0. Every robotic arm on a factory floor is first designed, simulated, and validated using joint constraints before physical prototyping. The **Grübler count** tells the engineer whether the mechanism can actually move (M > 0), is locked (M = 0), or is over-constrained and physically unrealizable (M < 0).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: DOF Analysis of a Slider-Crank Mechanism

The slider-crank is the **most important example** for KTU exams. It converts rotary motion of the crank into reciprocating motion of the slider.

**Given:** A four-bar slider-crank mechanism with crank of length $r$ and connecting rod of length $L$.

**Step 1 — Identify the links and joints:**

The mechanism has the following components:
- Ground link (fixed cylinder block) → 1 link
- Crank (rotates about pivot) → 1 link
- Connecting rod (coupler) → 1 link
- Slider (piston) → 1 link
- **Total links** $n = 4$

**Step 2 — Identify the joint types and their DOF:**

- Joint $O$ between Ground and Crank → **Revolute** (1 DOF) → counts as $j_1$
- Joint $A$ between Crank and Connecting Rod → **Revolute** (1 DOF) → counts as $j_1$
- Joint $B$ between Connecting Rod and Slider → **Revolute** (1 DOF) → counts as $j_1$
- Joint between Slider and Ground → **Prismatic/Slider** (1 DOF) → counts as $j_1$

Therefore: $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$

**Step 3 — Apply Grübler's Equation (Planar):**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$$

$$M = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Interpretation:** The slider-crank has exactly **1 DOF**, meaning a single input (rotation of the crank) fully determines the motion of all other links. ✓

**Step 4 — Position Analysis of the Slider:**

Let the crank rotate by angle $\theta$ from the horizontal axis. The crank end $A$ is at position:

$$\mathbf{r}_{OA} = (r\cos\theta, \, r\sin\theta, \, 0)$$

Let the connecting rod make an angle $\phi$ with the horizontal. The connecting rod end $B$ (slider position) is at:

$$\mathbf{r}_{OB} = \mathbf{r}_{OA} + (L\cos\phi, \, L\sin\phi, \, 0)$$

Since the slider moves only along the X-axis, the Y-coordinate must be zero:

$$r\sin\theta + L\sin\phi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = -\frac{r\sin\theta}{L}$$

The slider position along X:

$$x = r\cos\theta + L\cos\phi$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\cos\phi$ using the identity $\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi = 1$:**

$$\cos\phi = \pm\sqrt{1 - \frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{L^2}}$$

$$\cos\phi = \pm\frac{1}{L}\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form expression for slider position:**

$$x = r\cos\theta \pm \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$$

This is the equation that the CAD animation solver uses **frame-by-frame** to position the slider as the crank rotates. In SolidWorks Motion Study, this equation is solved automatically by the constraint solver.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: DOF Analysis of a Four-Bar Linkage (Whitworth Quick Return)

**Given:** A planar four-bar linkage with 4 links and 4 revolute joints.

**Step 1 — Count components:**
$n = 4$ links, $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints, $j_2 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Apply Grübler's Equation:**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$$

$$M = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Interpretation:** A four-bar mechanism has **1 DOF**. The driver crank rotation uniquely determines the coupler and follower positions. This is the famous **Grashof condition** mechanism, where:

$$S + L \leq P + Q$$

where $S$ = shortest link, $L$ = longest link, $P, Q$ = intermediate links. If this holds, the mechanism can achieve full continuous rotation (Crank-Rocker, Double-Crank, etc.).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Animation Time-Step and Servo Motor Equation

In CAD animation, a **Servo Motor** drives a joint by prescribing a time-varying position.

**Linear Servo Motor (Constant Velocity):**

Position as a function of time:

$$s(t) = s_0 + v \cdot t$$

where:
- $s_0$ = Initial position (mm)
- $v$ = Linear velocity (mm/s)
- $t$ = Time (s)

**Step-by-step frame update at time step $\Delta t$:**

Frame $i$ position:

$$s_i = s_0 + v \cdot (i \cdot \Delta t)$$

For $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$ where $N$ is the total number of frames.

**Example calculation:** Let $s_0 = 0$ mm, $v = 10$ mm/s, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, $N = 50$ frames.

- Frame 0: $s_0 = 0 + 10 \cdot 0 = 0$ mm
- Frame 1: $s_1 = 0 + 10 \cdot 0.1 = 1$ mm
- Frame 2: $s_2 = 0 + 10 \cdot 0.2 = 2$ mm
- ...
- Frame 49: $s_{49} = 0 + 10 \cdot 4.9 = 49$ mm

**Sinusoidal Servo Motor (Oscillating Cam):**

$$s(t) = s_0 + A\sin(2\pi f t)$$

where:
- $A$ = Amplitude (mm)
- $f$ = Frequency (Hz)
- $2\pi f = \omega$ = Angular frequency (rad/s)

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Joint DOF Reduction Table

This matrix is the **single most important table** for KTU lab viva and theory exams.

| Joint Type | Translation $T_x$ | Translation $T_y$ | Translation $T_z$ | Rotation $R_x$ | Rotation $R_y$ | Rotation $R_z$ | Remaining DOF |
|------------|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:-------------:|
| **Unconstrained** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 6 |
| **Coincident (Planes)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | 2 |
| **Concentric** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | 2 |
| **Distance Mate** | partial | partial | partial | partial | partial | partial | 1 controlled |
| **Angle Mate** | partial | partial | partial | partial | partial | partial | 1 controlled |
| **Revolute (Hinge)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | 1 (rot) |
| **Slider (Prismatic)** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | 1 (trans) |
| **Cylindrical** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | 2 (rot+trans) |
| **Ball (Spherical)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 3 (rot only) |
| **Planar** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | 3 (in plane) |
| **Rigid/Lock** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | 0 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Joint Creation Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete workflow for creating joints in a typical CAD assembly environment, as performed in the **PCRAL407 lab experiment**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Start Assembly Module] --> B[Step2 Import All Part Files]
    B --> C[Step3 Move Components to Rough Position]
    C --> D{Step4 Select Two Components}
    D --> E[Step5 Identify Reference Geometries]
    E --> F[Step6 Open Joint Manager Palette]
    F --> G{Step7 Choose Joint Type}
    G --> G1[Coincident Option]
    G --> G2[Concentric Option]
    G --> G3[Revolute Hinge Option]
    G --> G4[Slider Prismatic Option]
    G --> G5[Distance Angle Option]
    G1 --> H[Step8 Apply Joint]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I{Step9 Check DOF Indicator}
    I --> J[DOF Greater Than 0: Add More Joints]
    I --> K[DOF Equal to 0: Fully Constrained]
    I --> L[DOF Less Than 0: Over Constrained Error]
    J --> D
    K --> M[Step10 Open Motion Study Tab]
    L --> N[Step11 Delete Redundant Joint]
    N --> D
    M --> O[Step12 Apply Servo Motor or Force]
    O --> P[Step13 Set Time Duration and Step Size]
    P --> Q[Step14 Click Calculate]
    Q --> R[Step15 Play Animation to Verify Motion]
    R --> S{Step16 Motion Correct}
    S -- No --> O
    S -- Yes --> T[Step17 Export AVI or Save Assembly]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Animation Solver Pipeline

The internal computational pipeline of the CAD animation engine is represented below. This is a block-level functional architecture mapping, not a physical drawing, in accordance with the protocol for software-process topics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Input Servo Motor] --> B[Servo Motor Block]
    B --> C[Position Function s of t]
    C --> D[Joint Constraint Equations]
    D --> E[Constraint Jacobian J q dot]
    E --> F[Linear Algebra Solver]
    F --> G[New Generalized Coordinates q]
    G --> H[Transformation Matrix T 4x4]
    H --> I[Update All Component Poses]
    I --> J[Render Next Frame]
    J --> K{Simulation Time Over}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Output Animation File]
```

### 4.3 Joint Hierarchy in a Slider-Crank Assembly (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ground Block Cylinder] -->|Revolute Joint O| B[Crank Shaft]
    B -->|Revolute Joint A| C[Connecting Rod]
    C -->|Revolute Joint B| D[Piston Slider]
    D -->|Slider Joint C| A
    E[SolidWorks Motion Study Engine] -->|Drives Joint O| B
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** In the block view, each component (Ground, Crank, Connecting Rod, Piston) is represented as a node. The arrows show the joint connecting them. The "Servo Motor" in the centre is the driver that initiates and sustains motion in the assembly.

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Joint Type] --> B{What is the Desired Motion}
    B -->|Pure Rotation| C[Use Revolute Hinge Joint]
    B -->|Pure Translation| D[Use Slider Prismatic Joint]
    B -->|Rotation and Translation| E[Use Cylindrical Joint]
    B -->|Multi axis Rotation| F[Use Ball Spherical Joint]
    B -->|No Motion at All| G[Use Rigid Lock Joint]
    B -->|Controlled Distance| H[Use Distance Mate]
    B -->|Controlled Angle| I[Use Angle Mate]
    C --> J[Apply Concentric plus Coincident]
    D --> K[Apply Edge to Plane Alignment]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term "Assembly Joint" in CAD. Differentiate between a Revolute Joint and a Slider Joint with one engineering example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An assembly joint is a parametric constraint that defines the geometric relationship and permissible relative motion between two or more components in a CAD assembly. It mathematically restricts certain degrees of freedom (DOF) of the constrained bodies.
- **Revolute Joint (1 mark):** A revolute joint (also called a hinge or pin joint) allows **only 1 rotational DOF** about a common axis. The two components share a common centre and axis, and can only rotate relative to each other. *Example:* A door hinge, the pivot between a crank and the engine block, the elbow joint of a robot arm.
- **Slider Joint (1 mark):** A slider (prismatic) joint allows **only 1 translational DOF** along a single axis. The two components can slide linearly relative to each other but cannot rotate. *Example:* A piston moving inside a cylinder, a drawer sliding in a cabinet, a linear actuator rod.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
> **What is meant by "Degrees of Freedom (DOF)" of a mechanism? Mention the maximum DOF of a single rigid body in 3D space.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **DOF Definition (1.5 marks):** The number of Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a mechanism, also called its *mobility*, is the minimum number of independent inputs (coordinates) required to completely define the configuration of the mechanism. It is calculated using the Grübler's equation.
- **Maximum DOF in 3D (1.5 marks):** In 3-dimensional space, a single unconstrained rigid body has a maximum of **6 DOF** — comprising **3 translations** along the $X, Y, Z$ axes $(T_x, T_y, T_z)$ and **3 rotations** about the $X, Y, Z$ axes $(R_x, R_y, R_z)$. For planar (2D) mechanisms, the maximum DOF reduces to **3** (2 translations + 1 rotation in the plane).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure to create joints between two components in a CAD assembly. State any four commonly used joint types with their DOF characteristics. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A four-bar planar linkage consists of 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Using Grübler's equation, calculate the mobility. If one of the revolute joints is replaced by a prismatic joint, what will be the new mobility? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q3(a):**

**Block Diagram / Procedure (4 Marks):**

The procedure to create joints in a typical CAD assembly (such as SolidWorks) is as follows:

1. **Open the Assembly Module** of the CAD software.
2. **Insert/Import** all the required part files (.SLDPRT, .STEP, .IGES, .X_T) into the assembly workspace.
3. **Roughly position** the components using the *Move Component* tool.
4. **Select the first reference geometry** on Part 1 (e.g., a cylindrical face, a planar face, or an edge).
5. **Hold Ctrl and select the second reference geometry** on Part 2.
6. **Open the Joint Manager** (Mate Manager in older versions) from the Assembly tab.
7. **Choose the appropriate joint type** from the pop-up palette.
8. **Click "Apply"** or the green tick mark to confirm.
9. **Verify the DOF indicator** in the lower-right corner — it should decrease.
10. **Repeat** until DOF = 0 for a fully constrained assembly (or DOF = desired mobility for a mechanism).

**Four Commonly Used Joints (3 Marks):**

1. **Coincident Joint:** Aligns two planar faces to lie in the same plane. Constrains 4 DOF, leaves 2.
2. **Concentric Joint:** Aligns the axes of two cylindrical/circular faces. Constrains 4 DOF, leaves 2 (1 rotation + 1 translation along the axis).
3. **Revolute Joint:** A combination of coincident + concentric that allows only 1 rotational DOF about the shared axis.
4. **Slider (Prismatic) Joint:** Allows only 1 translational DOF along a specified direction. Constrains 5 DOF, leaves 1.

**Model Solution for Q3(b):**

**Case 1 — Original four-bar with 4 revolute joints (4 Marks):**

Given: $n = 4$ links, $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints (lower pairs), $j_2 = 0$.

Applying Grübler's planar equation:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$$

$$M = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Mobility = 1 DOF** — the mechanism can be driven by a single input.

**Case 2 — One revolute joint replaced by a prismatic joint (3 Marks):**

Prismatic joints are also lower pairs (1 DOF each), so $j_1$ count remains 4.

The change in $j_1$ count: $j_1 = 4$ (3 revolute + 1 prismatic).

Therefore:

$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Mobility is still 1 DOF.** The substitution of a revolute joint with a prismatic joint does not change the mobility count, because both are 1-DOF lower pairs. However, the *type* of motion (rotational vs. translational) at that joint changes, altering the mechanism's functional behaviour. This is the basis of the **Inverted Slider-Crank** mechanism.

---

> **Q4. Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of "Motion Study" in CAD. List the three types of motion studies available in SolidWorks and state the difference between animation and basic motion. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A slider-crank mechanism has a crank of length 100 mm and a connecting rod of length 300 mm. If the crank rotates at 120 RPM, calculate the maximum and minimum slider velocity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q4(a):**

**Concept of Motion Study (2 Marks):**
A **Motion Study** is a CAD environment feature that simulates the kinematic and dynamic behaviour of an assembly over time, based on the joint constraints defined in the assembly. It allows engineers to validate the design for interference, range of motion, and operational feasibility *before* manufacturing a physical prototype.

**Three Types of Motion Studies in SolidWorks (3 Marks):**

1. **Animation:** Keyframe-based motion where the user manually sets component positions at specific time steps. Best for visual presentation and concept demos.
2. **Basic Motion:** Uses the physical solver to compute positions based on motors, springs, and dampers. Includes mass, inertia, and gravity. Suitable for simple dynamic validation.
3. **Motion Analysis (SolidWorks Premium):** A full FEA-coupled dynamic simulation that computes forces, contact, friction, and stresses. Suitable for engineering validation.

**Difference between Animation and Basic Motion (2 Marks):**
- **Animation** uses *interpolation* between user-defined keyframes — it does not respect physics. The slider can pass through walls.
- **Basic Motion** uses the *physics solver* — it respects mass, gravity, contact, and joint constraints. Components cannot interpenetrate, and motion is calculated using Newton's laws and the constraint Jacobian.

**Model Solution for Q4(b):**

**Given:**
- Crank length $r = 100$ mm
- Connecting rod length $L = 300$ mm
- Crank speed $N = 120$ RPM

**Step 1 — Convert crank speed to angular velocity (1 Mark):**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 120}{60} = 4\pi \approx 12.566 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Write the slider position equation (2 Marks):**

$$x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to get velocity (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{dx}{d\theta} = -r\sin\theta + \frac{-2r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta}{2\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}$$

$$\frac{dx}{d\theta} = -r\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}$$

The slider velocity is:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \omega \cdot \frac{dx}{d\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Find extreme velocities (1 Mark):**

Using the standard slider-crank result, the approximate max and min velocities are:

$$v_{max} = \omega \left(r + \frac{r^2}{2L}\right) = 4\pi \left(0.1 + \frac{0.01}{0.6}\right)$$

$$v_{max} = 4\pi \times 0.1167 \approx 1.466 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{min} = \omega \left(r - \frac{r^2}{2L}\right) = 4\pi \times 0.0833 \approx 1.047 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer (1 Mark):**
- **Maximum slider velocity** $\approx$ **1.47 m/s**
- **Minimum slider velocity** $\approx$ **1.05 m/s**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s** — this is the #1 mistake. Always use $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> 2. **Confusing "Mate" and "Joint"** in viva — KTU 2024 expects the modern term *Joint*. Students using legacy terminology may lose 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring the DOF check** — the question may ask "is the mechanism movable?" If $M \leq 0$, state that explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing planar (3n) and spatial (6n) equations** — confirm whether the mechanism is 2D or 3D before applying Grübler.
> 5. **Missing units in the final answer** — KTU strictly penalizes missing units like mm, rad/s, m/s.
> 6. **Not stating the joint types in (a)** — examiners allocate marks specifically for naming the joint, not just the procedure.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Joint = Constraint = Mate** (all three refer to the same CAD concept).
- [x] A single rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** ($3T + 3R$).
- [x] A rigid/lock joint removes **all 6 DOF**.
- [x] A **revolute joint** allows exactly **1 DOF** (rotation only).
- [x] A **slider (prismatic) joint** allows exactly **1 DOF** (translation only).
- [x] A **cylindrical joint** allows **2 DOF** (rotation + translation along same axis).
- [x] A **ball (spherical) joint** allows **3 DOF** (3 rotations, no translation).
- [x] **Grübler Planar:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- [x] **Grübler Spatial:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- [x] Slider-crank mobility = **1 DOF** (n=4, j₁=4).
- [x] Four-bar linkage mobility = **1 DOF** (n=4, j₁=4).
- [x] **Motion Study** types: Animation, Basic Motion, Motion Analysis.
- [x] **Servo Motor** is the standard input driver for motion studies.
- [x] Animation **does not respect physics**; Basic Motion **does**.
- [x] **Over-constrained assembly** (DOF < 0) must be corrected by removing redundant joints.
- [x] In lab record, always include **screenshots** of the joint manager, the DOF indicator, and the final animated assembly.
- [x] Always export the motion study as **.AVI** or **.MP4** for submission along with the **.SLDASM** file.
- [x] For KTU viva, be ready to explain the **physical meaning** of the Grübler mobility value (M=1 means one motor needed).
- [x] Standard time step $\Delta t$ for lab animations: **0.1 s**; total duration: **3–5 seconds**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic simulation of four bar mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELLING AND SIMULATION OF MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Kinematic simulation of four bar mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **four-bar mechanism** (also called a **four-bar linkage** or **quadric crank chain**) is the simplest movable closed-chain planar mechanism consisting of **four rigid links** connected in a loop by **four revolute (pin) joints**, with one link designated as the *ground* (frame).

The four links are conventionally named as follows:

| Symbol | Link Name | Function | Length |
|:------:|:---------:|:--------:|:------:|
| $r_1$ | Ground / Frame | Fixed reference link | $L_1$ |
| $r_2$ | Input Link (Crank) | Driver link providing motion | $L_2$ |
| $r_3$ | Coupler (Connecting Rod) | Floating link transferring motion | $L_3$ |
| $r_4$ | Output Link (Rocker / Follower) | Driven link performing useful work | $L_4$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinematic simulation** refers to the computational determination of the **position, velocity, and acceleration** of every point on the mechanism at successive instants of time, typically by numerically solving the loop-closure equations at discrete steps of the input crank angle.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a sewing machine needle mechanism or a vehicle windshield wiper.**
